sourceText: Microsoft is giving away for free what every wannabe compliance tool provider is pitching as a platform right now. Seven packages. MIT license. Runtime policy enforcement. OWASP evidence collection. EU AI Act compliance mapping. Framework-agnostic. The same feature list that costs you a monthly subscription, a sales call, and an overpriced SLA, open-sourced on April 2. Here is what neither has solved. The problem is not technical. It is structural. An agent with ten authorized tools across ten chaining steps produces ten billion possible workflows. If you cannot bound the behavioral space, and the math says you cannot, then continuous monitoring is watching infinity with a dashboard. You are automating compliance against a broken baseline. At sub-millisecond latency. When an agent dynamically enters a high-risk domain nobody scoped, the deployer inherits full provider obligations under the Act. The liability transfer happens in the regulation, not in the runtime. The tooling is not the bottleneck. The architecture is. Using an agent to solve the agent governance problem is like asking the defendant to prosecute itself.
draft: This distinction matters. Action-level enforcement is necessary, but it is not the whole governance problem. An agent can stay inside the boundary on each individual step and still drift outside the intended workflow outcome across the chain. The harder layer is reconstructible decision context: original intent, scoped authority, action sequence, approval path, and evidence of why each step was allowed. That is the direction I’m building around with Agent Action Gate: pre-execution decisions, receipts, audit, locked policy governance, MetaGate, and Workflow Scope Ledger.
